skill.name: ena-database
skill.description: Access European Nucleotide Archive APIs and FTP resources for nucleotide sequences, raw reads, assemblies, taxonomy, and metadata retrieval.
skill.summary: Query ENA accessions, metadata, taxonomy, sequence records, and bulk download links for genomics workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, sensitive-data, weak-crypto, and network patterns, but review found these are documentation examples for the European Nucleotide Archive. The real behavior is intended network access to public ENA APIs, FTP links, and optional ENA command-line tools; no prompt injection, credential theft, or malicious execution intent was found.

low_findings.title: Intended Public ENA Network Access
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for network behavior. The skill intentionally documents requests to public ENA API, FTP, taxonomy, browser, and cross-reference endpoints. This is expected for the skill, but users should understand that prompts may cause outbound requests and large dataset downloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly define ENA endpoints and request examples. The network access aligns with the stated genomics data retrieval purpose.

low_findings.title: Optional ENA Command-Line Download Guidance
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for external command references, but low risk in context. The command examples use ENA download utilities for public accession retrieval and are documentation snippets, not code executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines name enaBrowserTools and show fixed utility examples using accession IDs. I found no shell interpolation, hidden script, or automatic command execution.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive and Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for Windows SAM, C2, and weak-crypto findings. The scanner matched biological accession prefixes such as SAMEA, MD5 checksum documentation for FASTQ and CRAM references, and ordinary HTTP status code text rather than credential stores or malicious control logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are embedded in ENA metadata, checksum lookup, and API error handling documentation. No evidence found of credential access, cryptographic security decisions, or command-and-control behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are Documentation Syntax
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for most Ruby or shell backtick detections. The flagged backticks delimit Markdown inline code, tables, URLs, endpoint names, and Python examples instead of invoking a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown documentation and the cited backticks are formatting markers. No executable Ruby code or shell backtick evaluation exists in the reviewed context.

content.value_statement: Genomics teams need reliable access to ENA records, files, and metadata. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through ENA APIs, FTP links, taxonomy lookups, and accession-based retrieval.
actual_capabilities: Explains ENA Portal API searches for studies, samples, runs, assemblies, sequences, analyses, and taxa.
Shows how to retrieve XML, FASTA, text, file reports, and tabular metadata by accession.
Documents taxonomy, cross-reference, and CRAM reference registry endpoints.
Guides FASTQ and assembly download planning through FTP, Aspera, and enaBrowserTools references.
Provides query syntax, pagination, field selection, rate-limit handling, and retry patterns.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include a packaged executable client.
It depends on ENA service availability, response formats, and public endpoint limits.
Large downloads may require local storage planning and external transfer tools.
It does not validate biological interpretation or downstream analysis quality.

use_cases.title: Prepare Sequence Retrieval
use_cases.description: Find the correct ENA endpoint and format for a known accession before analysis.

use_cases.title: Build Metadata Searches
use_cases.description: Construct Portal API queries for studies, samples, runs, assemblies, and taxonomic filters.

use_cases.title: Plan Bulk Downloads
use_cases.description: Identify FASTQ file reports, FTP links, checksums, and bulk transfer options for pipeline input.

prompt_templates.title: Look Up an Accession
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ENA database skill to explain which endpoint I should use for accession ERR123456 and what output formats are available.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Study Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ENA database skill to build a Portal API query for all samples in study PRJNA123456. Include recommended fields and format.

prompt_templates.title: Plan FASTQ Downloads
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ENA database skill to outline how to get FASTQ download URLs, file sizes, and checksums for run ERR123456.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Robust Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ENA database skill to design a rate-limited workflow that searches assemblies by taxonomy, paginates results, and caches taxonomy lookups.

output_examples.input: Find metadata for a sequencing run accession.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the Browser API for direct metadata and the Portal file report for download links, formats, file sizes, and checksums.

output_examples.input: Search for assemblies for a bacterial organism.
output_examples.output: The response recommends a Portal API assembly search with a taxonomy tree filter, selected metadata fields, JSON or TSV output, and pagination.

output_examples.input: Prepare a bulk FASTQ retrieval plan.
output_examples.output: The response lists the file report step, FTP or Aspera transfer options, checksum verification, rate-limit handling, and local storage considerations.

best_practices: Use specific result types and fields to reduce response size and parsing work.
Handle HTTP 429 responses with backoff and avoid unnecessary repeated requests.
Record study, sample, run, and assembly accessions for reproducible citation.
anti_patterns: Do not scrape ENA web pages when documented APIs provide the same data.
Do not iterate unbounded accession lists without pagination, delays, and storage checks.
Do not parse XML or tabular responses with fragile regular expressions.

faq.question: Does this skill download data by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for using ENA APIs, FTP links, and ENA download tools.

faq.question: Which ENA data types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers studies, samples, raw reads, assemblies, sequences, analyses, taxonomy records, and cross-references.

faq.question: Can it help with FASTQ files?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains file reports, FASTQ FTP fields, checksums, file sizes, and bulk download options.

faq.question: Does it support taxonomy searches?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents taxon ID lookup, scientific name lookup, and taxonomy tree query patterns.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What are the main safety considerations?
faq.answer: The main considerations are outbound network access, FTP downloads, rate limits, and large file storage needs.
